questions.question_text: Which action constitutes unprofessional conduct for a Montana licensee?
questions.correct_answer: Failing to present all written offers
distractors: Declining to take an overpriced listing
Suggesting the seller make a counteroffer
questions.explanation: Montana requires a seller agent to submit all offers and counteroffers received — written or not — until a pending transaction closes or the listing terminates, unless the seller waives these obligations in writing (ARM 24.210.641(1)(n)). Failing to do so is unprofessional conduct, regardless of the offer amount or terms.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent tells buyers the property value will double in five years. What violation has occurred?
questions.explanation: Guaranteeing or promising future profits on real estate is unprofessional conduct in Montana. Licensees may share market data but must never guarantee specific investment returns or property appreciation.

questions.question_text: Agent Kim drafts a purchase addendum modifying legal obligations between buyer and seller. What violation is this?
questions.explanation: Drafting legal documents that modify legal rights and obligations between parties constitutes the unauthorized practice of law. Montana agents may fill in blanks on approved forms but cannot draft custom legal provisions.

questions.question_text: Agent Rivera accepts compensation from both the seller and a lender without telling either party. How many violations occurred?
distractors: One: only the lender payment counts
None if the amounts are reasonable
questions.explanation: Accepting, giving, or charging an undisclosed commission, rebate, or profit is unprofessional conduct (MCA 37-51-321(1)(f)) — each undisclosed source is its own failure, so both the seller-side and lender-side payments must be disclosed. The lender payment also implicates RESPA Section 8 if tied to settlement-service referrals. The amount is irrelevant; the failure to disclose is the violation.

questions.question_text: A listing agent places a SOLD sign on a property before any offer has been accepted. Which violation applies?
distractors: No violation if a sale is expected soon
questions.explanation: Advertising a property as sold when it is not misrepresents the property's status — intentionally misleading advertising under MCA 37-51-321(1)(a) and a misleading representation under MCA 37-1-316(1)(g). Montana's rules no longer contain a specific sign-placement provision; a related current rule, ARM 24.210.641(1)(i), also bars advising that an offer has been accepted without a signed document evidencing acceptance.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent receives a $500 gift card from a title company for each referral. What violation has occurred?
distractors: No violation if the agent discloses it
questions.explanation: Under RESPA Section 8, receiving anything of value in exchange for referrals to settlement service providers is an illegal kickback regardless of disclosure. The payment is tied directly to referral activity, making it a per se violation of federal law.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent advises a buyer that a contract clause is legally unenforceable. What violation is this?
questions.correct_answer: Providing legal advice without a license
distractors: A permitted service under agency duties
Only a violation if the advice is wrong
questions.explanation: Interpreting contract clauses and advising clients on legal enforceability constitutes the unauthorized practice of law. Montana agents may fill in approved forms but must refer legal questions to a licensed attorney.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent steers buyers to a specific lender because the lender pays the agent a referral bonus. What violation has occurred?
questions.explanation: Recommending a provider without disclosing a financial relationship or interest is unprofessional conduct (ARM 24.210.641(1)(e)), and an undisclosed commission or profit violates MCA 37-51-321(1)(f). Because the bonus is tied to settlement-service referrals, RESPA Section 8 also applies — and RESPA kickbacks are illegal even if disclosed.

questions.question_text: A licensee discovers a colleague is receiving undisclosed kickbacks from a warranty company. What should the licensee do?
distractors: Confront the colleague in private first
Wait until the transaction fully closes
questions.explanation: Anyone may file a complaint with the Board of Realty Regulation, and reporting suspected kickback arrangements protects consumers and the profession. Montana law does not impose an affirmative duty on licensees to report colleagues, but ignoring known misconduct, confronting privately, or waiting serves no protective purpose — filing a board complaint is the appropriate professional action.

questions.question_text: A Montana listing agent fails to inform the seller that a competing offer exists. What violation is this?
distractors: No violation if the offer was verbal
questions.explanation: Montana requires seller agents to submit all offers and counteroffers to the seller — the rule is not limited to written offers, and it applies until closing or listing termination unless the seller waives it in writing (ARM 24.210.641(1)(n)). Withholding a competing offer deprives the seller of the ability to make an informed decision and constitutes unprofessional conduct.

questions.question_text: Three competing brokerages agree to charge identical commission rates in Billings. What antitrust violation is this?
questions.explanation: When competing brokerages agree on commission rates, it constitutes price-fixing, a per se violation of federal antitrust law. Commissions must be individually and freely negotiated between broker and client.

questions.question_text: Several Missoula brokerages agree that none will cooperate with a new discount brokerage. What violation occurred?
distractors: A market allocation scheme by area
An illegal tying arrangement on services
questions.explanation: A collective refusal to deal with a competitor is a group boycott, which is a per se antitrust violation. Each brokerage must independently decide whether to cooperate with other firms.

questions.question_text: A Montana agent refuses to show homes in a neighborhood based on the buyer's national origin. Which law is violated?
questions.explanation: The Montana Human Rights Act prohibits discrimination in real estate transactions based on protected classes including national origin. This mirrors and supplements federal Fair Housing Act protections within Montana.

questions.question_text: Two brokerages divide Helena so each exclusively serves one geographic zone. What antitrust violation is this?
questions.explanation: Dividing geographic territories among competitors is market allocation, a per se antitrust violation. Brokerages must compete freely and cannot agree to divide markets by area, price range, or property type.

questions.question_text: A brokerage requires buyers to use its affiliated title company as a condition of representation. What violation is this?
questions.explanation: Requiring a client to purchase one service as a condition of receiving another is an illegal tying arrangement under antitrust law. Buyers must be free to choose their own settlement service providers.
